section.title: The Weeping Prophet
section.sectionRationale: This is the heart of Jeremiah's grief, the passage that has earned him the name the weeping prophet. Judah's wound proves incurable, false healers keep crying peace where there is no peace, and in the middle of the wreckage stands the one boast still open to sinners, to know the LORD who delights in loving devotion, justice, and righteousness.
section.thesis: Judah refuses to turn back despite warning after warning, and the coming judgment breaks even the prophet who announces it, yet true wisdom is not found in cleverness, strength, or wealth but only in knowing the LORD, whose own heart is set on steadfast love, justice, and righteousness.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Judah's stubborn refusal to repent and the false healers who cry peace where there is none; the prophet's own grief that there is no balm for so great a wound; his wish to weep without ceasing and God's charge of pervasive deceit; a summons to lament for the land God will make desolate; and the true boast, not in wisdom, strength, or wealth, but in knowing the LORD.

units.label: No one repents
units.note: false healers cry peace where there is none

verses.text:
So you are to tell them this is what the LORD says: 
“Do men fall and not get up again? Does one turn away and not return?
Why then have these people turned away? Why does Jerusalem always turn away? They cling to deceit; they refuse to return.
I have listened and heard; they do not speak what is right. No one repents of his wickedness, asking, ‘What have I done?’ Everyone has pursued his own course like a horse charging into battle.
Even the stork in the sky knows her appointed seasons. The turtledove, the swift, and the thrush keep their time of migration, but My people do not know the requirements of the LORD.
How can you say, ‘We are wise, and the Law of the LORD is with us,’ when in fact the lying pen of the scribes has produced a deception?
The wise will be put to shame; they will be dismayed and trapped. Since they have rejected the word of the LORD, what wisdom do they really have?
Therefore I will give their wives to other men and their fields to new owners. For from the least of them to the greatest, all are greedy for gain; from prophet to priest, all practice deceit.
They dress the wound of the daughter of My people with very little care, saying, ‘Peace, peace,’ when there is no peace at all.
I will take away their harvest, declares the LORD. There will be no grapes on the vine, nor figs on the tree, and even the leaf will wither. Whatever I have given them will be lost to them.”

units.label: Is there no balm in Gilead?

verses.text: Why are we just sitting here? Gather together, let us flee to the fortified cities and perish there, for the LORD our God has doomed us. He has given us poisoned water to drink, because we have sinned against the LORD.
We hoped for peace, but no good has come, for a time of healing, but there was only terror.

The snorting of enemy horses is heard from Dan. At the sound of the neighing of mighty steeds, the whole land quakes. They come to devour the land and everything in it, the city and all who dwell in it.

“For behold, I will send snakes among you, vipers that cannot be charmed, 
and they will bite you,” declares the LORD.
My sorrow is beyond healing; my heart is faint within me.

Listen to the cry of the daughter of my people from a land far away: 
“Is the LORD no longer in Zion? Is her King no longer there?” 
“Why have they provoked Me to anger with their carved images, 
with their worthless foreign idols?”
“The harvest has passed, the summer has ended, but we have not been saved.”
For the brokenness of the daughter of my people I am crushed. I mourn; horror has gripped me.
Is there no {{tsori|balm}} in Gilead? Is no physician there? Why then has the health of the daughter of my people not been restored?

units.label: Oh, that my head were a spring of water
units.note: a wish to weep without ceasing

verses.text: Oh, that my head were a spring of water, and my eyes a fountain of tears! I would {{bakah|weep}} day and night over the slain daughter of my people.
If only I had a traveler’s lodge in the wilderness, I would abandon my people and depart from them, for they are all adulterers, a crowd of faithless people.
“They bend their tongues like bows; lies prevail over truth in the land. For they proceed from evil to evil, and they do not take Me into account,” declares the LORD.
“Let everyone guard against his neighbor; do not trust any brother, for every brother deals craftily, and every friend spreads slander.
Each one betrays his friend; no one tells the truth. They have taught their tongues to lie; they wear themselves out committing iniquity.
You dwell in the midst of deception; in their deceit they refuse to know Me,” declares the LORD.

Therefore this is what the LORD of Hosts says: 
“Behold, I will refine them and test them, for what else can I do 
because of the daughter of My people?
Their tongues are deadly arrows; they speak deception. With his mouth a man speaks peace to his neighbor, but in his heart he sets a trap for him.
Should I not punish them for these things? declares the LORD. Should I not avenge Myself on such a nation as this?”

units.label: A lament for the land
units.note: wailing women summoned, corpses like dung

verses.text: I will take up a weeping and wailing for the mountains, a dirge over the wilderness pasture, for they have been scorched so no one passes through, and the lowing of cattle is not heard. Both the birds of the air and the beasts have fled; they have gone away.
“And I will make Jerusalem a heap of rubble, a haunt for jackals; and I will make the cities of Judah a desolation, without inhabitant.”
Who is the man wise enough to understand this? To whom has the mouth of the LORD spoken, that he may explain it? Why is the land destroyed and scorched like a desert, so no one can pass through it?
And the LORD answered, “It is because they have forsaken My law, which I set before them; they have not walked in it or obeyed My voice.
Instead, they have followed the stubbornness of their hearts and gone after the Baals, as their fathers taught them.”
Therefore this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: “Behold, I will feed this people wormwood and give them poisoned water to drink.
I will scatter them among the nations that neither they nor their fathers have known, and I will send a sword after them until I have finished them off.”

This is what the LORD of Hosts says: 
“Take note, and summon the wailing women; send for the most skillful among them.
Let them come quickly and take up a lament over us, that our eyes may overflow with tears, and our eyelids may gush with water.
For the sound of wailing is heard from Zion: ‘How devastated we are! How great is our shame! For we have abandoned the land because our dwellings have been torn down.’”
Now, O women, hear the word of the LORD. Open your ears to the word of His mouth. Teach your daughters to wail, and one another to lament.
For death has climbed in through our windows; it has entered our fortresses to cut off the children from the streets, the young men from the town squares.

Declare that this is what the LORD says: 
“The corpses of men will fall like dung upon the open field, like newly cut grain behind the reaper, with no one to gather it.”

units.label: Let him who boasts, boast in this
units.note: true wisdom is knowing the LORD

verses.text:
This is what the LORD says: 
“Let not the wise man {{halal|boast}} in his wisdom, nor the strong man in his strength, 
nor the wealthy man in his riches.

But let him who boasts boast in this, that he understands and knows Me, that I am the LORD, who exercises loving devotion, justice and righteousness on the earth, for I delight in these things,” declares the LORD.
“Behold, the days are coming,” declares the LORD, “when I will punish all who are circumcised yet uncircumcised:

Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and all the inhabitants of the desert who clip the hair of their temples. For all these nations are uncircumcised, and the whole house of Israel is {{arel|uncircumcised}} in heart.”

tsori.gloss: Balm, a soothing resin used medicinally and prized in trade, associated with Gilead. Jeremiah's question exposes that Judah's wound goes past any earthly remedy; only the LORD Himself can heal what sin has broken.

bakah.gloss: To weep, to cry out in grief. Jeremiah's wish for a fountain of tears earns him the title the weeping prophet; his tears are not a failure of faith but the shape lived faith takes when covenant love meets covenant unfaithfulness.

halal.gloss: To boast or to praise; the same root gives Israel its word for praise, hallelujah. Here God redirects all boasting away from wisdom, strength, and riches to the one ground of true praise, knowing Him.

arel.gloss: Uncircumcised, used literally of the flesh and, startlingly, of the heart. Judah kept the sign of the covenant in the flesh while its heart remained untouched by God, showing the sign was always meant to point to a transformed inner life.

basic.context: Jeremiah has just delivered his temple sermon; now he turns inward and lets the grief of coming judgment wash over his own heart. Judah refuses to repent though the whole created order, even migrating birds, keeps its appointed times better than God's people know His requirements. False prophets and priests dress the nation's wound with a shallow, Peace, peace, when there is no peace, and Jeremiah is undone with sorrow, wishing his eyes were a fountain of tears.
basic.hermeneutics: This is poetry of lament woven through prophetic oracle so tightly that at points it is genuinely hard to tell whether Jeremiah or the LORD is speaking, or both at once. That is not a flaw; it shows how fully the prophet's heart has been shaped by God's own grief over His people. The passage also contains one of Scripture's most quoted lines on true boasting, verses 23 and 24, which Paul later picks up almost word for word.
basic.originalLanguages: Jeremiah asks whether there is tsori, balm, in Gilead, a region famous for a soothing medicinal resin, exposing that Judah's wound has gone past any remedy trade could supply. He wishes he could bakah, weep, day and night, giving him his lasting name, the weeping prophet. God redirects all halal, boasting, away from wisdom, strength, and wealth to knowing Him alone, and calls Judah arel, uncircumcised, in heart, even though the flesh still bore the covenant sign.
basic.typology: Jeremiah's tears over an unrepentant Jerusalem anticipate Jesus weeping over the same city centuries later. The impossible wound with no balm anticipates the true balm and physician, Christ, who alone heals what sin has broken. And the call to boast only in knowing the LORD anticipates the gospel logic Paul later makes explicit, that all boasting is excluded except in Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus stands in the line of Jeremiah as the weeping prophet magnified: He weeps over Jerusalem's hardness of heart, Luke 19:41-44, in language that echoes a physician grieving over a patient he cannot heal, recalling Jeremiah's balm in Gilead. Paul takes up Jeremiah 9:23-24 almost word for word in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17: let him who boasts, boast in the Lord, because in Christ alone believers have wisdom, righteousness, and redemption to boast in.
basic.god: God is grieved by His people's sin in a way that defies easy categories; His words and Jeremiah's tears blend until the reader can no longer separate them. Yet in the middle of announced judgment God names what He delights in: loving devotion, justice, and righteousness on the earth. Even the harshest oracle in this passage serves the character God wants known and praised.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 19:41-44 has Jesus weep over Jerusalem much as Jeremiah weeps here. 1 Corinthians 1:26-31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17 quote Jeremiah 9:23-24 directly. Deuteronomy 10:16 and Romans 2:28-29 speak of circumcision of the heart, the very thing Judah lacked. Matthew 9:12 pictures Jesus as the physician the sick actually need.
basic.oneStory: This is the anguish of a shepherd's heart over a people who will not turn, carried by a prophet whose tears mirror God's own. Jesus is the greater weeping prophet, who weeps over the same city and provides the balm sin cannot heal on its own, so that the one boast left to sinners, knowing the LORD, becomes, in Christ, boasting in the wisdom, righteousness, and redemption He alone supplies.

context.literary: This unit continues the indictment begun in the temple sermon, 7:1-8:3, moving from public rebuke to inward lament; it is often grouped with 8:4-10:25 as material substantiating God's case against Judah's hypocrisy and idolatry.
context.historical: Likely proclaimed in the years leading up to Babylon's invasions under Jehoiakim, when Judah's leaders, prophets, and priests alike offered false assurances of peace even as the danger from the north grew.
context.canonical: This passage gives Jeremiah his enduring title, the weeping prophet, and supplies the seedbed for the book of Lamentations. Its climax in 9:23-24 becomes one of the most quoted Old Testament verses in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose Judah's persistent, willful refusal to repent despite abundant warning, to let the prophet's own grief model how God's messengers should feel the weight of the judgment they proclaim, and to relocate all human boasting away from wisdom, strength, and wealth to the sole knowledge of the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The lament describes a specific historical crisis and Jeremiah's personal anguish within it; it does not prescribe despair as an end in itself. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that no achievement gives a right to boast before God, only knowing Him does, and that grief over sin is a faithful, not a faithless, response.

debates.question: In 8:18 to 9:1, whose voice is weeping, Jeremiah's or the LORD's?

views.label: Jeremiah's own personal lament
views.case: The first person language, my sorrow, my heart, most naturally reads as the prophet's own grief over his people, distinct from the divine speech surrounding it.

views.label: The LORD's grief voiced through the prophet
views.case: The lines shift without clear markers between God's oracles and this lament, suggesting the prophet's tears are themselves an expression of God's own pathos over Judah, given human voice.

debates.bearing: Either reading teaches the same lesson: a true prophet's heart is conformed to God's heart, so that proclaiming judgment and weeping over it are never in tension but flow from the same love.

study.originalLanguages: Jeremiah's question, is there tsori, balm, in Gilead, exposes that Judah's wound has gone past any remedy trade could supply, since Gilead was famous precisely for this soothing resin. His wish to bakah, weep, day and night gives him his lasting name, the weeping prophet, showing that grief belongs to faithful ministry, not merely to failure. God relocates all halal, boasting, away from wisdom, strength, and wealth to knowing Him alone, and calls Judah arel, uncircumcised, in heart, even though the flesh still bore the covenant sign, exposing that the sign was always meant to point to a transformed heart.

typology.type: Jeremiah weeping over an unrepentant Jerusalem, Jeremiah 8:18-9:1
typology.fulfillment: Jesus weeps over the same city's hardness of heart, Luke 19:41-44, the weeping prophet's grief fulfilled and deepened in the incarnate Son.

typology.type: The wound with no balm, Jeremiah 8:22
typology.fulfillment: Christ is revealed as the true physician and balm for sin that no earthly remedy can heal, Matthew 9:12.

typology.type: The command to boast only in knowing the LORD, Jeremiah 9:23-24
typology.fulfillment: Paul applies this directly to Christ: believers boast only in the Lord, who has become for them wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption, 1 Corinthians 1:30-31.

study.christ: Jesus stands in the line of Jeremiah as the weeping prophet magnified: He weeps over Jerusalem's hardness of heart, Luke 19:41-44, in words that echo a physician grieving over a patient he cannot heal, recalling Jeremiah's balm in Gilead. Paul takes up Jeremiah 9:23-24 almost word for word in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17, let him who boasts, boast in the Lord, because in Christ alone believers now have wisdom, righteousness, and redemption to boast in, the very boast this passage commands.
study.god: God is grieved by His people's sin in a way that defies easy categories; His words and Jeremiah's tears blend so closely that the reader can no longer separate them. Yet even here God names what He delights in: loving devotion, justice, and righteousness on the earth. His judgment on Judah's hollow religion, the sign of the covenant kept in flesh while the heart stayed untouched, shows He has always looked past ritual to the heart itself.

crossReferences.note: Jesus weeps over Jerusalem's coming judgment, echoing Jeremiah's own tears over the same city.
Paul draws directly on Jeremiah 9:23-24 to ground all Christian boasting in Christ alone.
Repeats the same principle, let him who boasts, boast in the Lord.
True circumcision is of the heart, the very transformation Judah lacked despite the outward sign.

study.oneStory: This is the anguish of a shepherd's heart over a people who will not turn, carried by a prophet whose tears mirror God's own. Jesus is the greater weeping prophet, who weeps over the same city and provides the balm sin cannot heal on its own, so that the one boast left to sinners, knowing the LORD, becomes, in Christ, boasting in the wisdom, righteousness, and redemption He alone supplies.

quiz.q: Why does Jeremiah say, is there no balm in Gilead?

options.text: Because Judah's wound from sin runs deeper than any earthly remedy can heal.
options.note: Verse 22 exposes that the nation's brokenness has gone past ordinary cure.

options.text: Because Gilead had literally run out of its famous medicinal resin.
options.note: The point is spiritual, not a literal shortage of trade goods.

options.text: Because Jeremiah had never actually visited Gilead.
options.note: The question is rhetorical, not about Jeremiah's travel history.

options.text: Because the coming exile would only be a temporary hardship.
options.note: The lament actually stresses that the wound is severe and unhealed, not minor.

quiz.q: What false comfort do the prophets and priests offer in 8:11?

options.text: They dress the wound of the people lightly, saying, Peace, peace, when there is no peace.
options.note: Verse 11 names this exact false reassurance.

options.text: They call the people to a public fast.
options.note: Fasting is not mentioned here; false comfort through empty words is the issue.

options.text: They warn the people honestly of coming judgment.
options.note: The opposite; their words are deceptively comforting, not honest warning.

options.text: They promise a swift military victory over Babylon.
options.note: The text speaks of false healing of a wound, a broader image than a specific military promise.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah wish for in 9:1?

options.text: That his head were a spring of water and his eyes a fountain of tears, to weep without ceasing over his people.
options.note: This wish gives Jeremiah his enduring title, the weeping prophet.

options.text: That he had never been called as a prophet at all.
options.note: That despair is not expressed here; his tears flow from love for his people, not regret over his calling.

options.text: That God would immediately destroy Judah's enemies.
options.note: Jeremiah's wish here is grief, not a call for vengeance.

options.text: That he could find a way to warn the people more persuasively.
options.note: Close to the spirit of the passage, but the specific wish in verse 1 is to weep, not to find better arguments.

quiz.q: According to 9:23-24, in what alone should a person boast?

options.text: In understanding and knowing the LORD, who delights in loving devotion, justice, and righteousness.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 redirect all boasting away from wisdom, strength, and wealth to knowing God.

options.text: In wisdom gained through long study and learning.
options.note: Verse 23 explicitly excludes boasting in wisdom as the ultimate ground.

options.text: In personal strength and courage.
options.note: Verse 23 explicitly excludes boasting in strength.

options.text: In accumulated wealth and riches.
options.note: Verse 23 explicitly excludes boasting in riches.

quiz.q: What does God mean by calling Israel uncircumcised in heart in 9:25-26?

options.text: That they kept the outward covenant sign while their hearts remained untransformed and unfaithful.
options.note: The point is that the sign of the covenant was never meant to replace an inward, transformed loyalty to God.

options.text: That Israel had literally abandoned the practice of circumcision.
options.note: The charge concerns the heart, not a claim that the physical rite had been discontinued.

options.text: That circumcision was no longer required by God at all.
options.note: The passage does not abolish the sign; it exposes a heart that never matched the sign.

options.text: That only the surrounding nations needed any kind of circumcision.
options.note: The passage groups Israel with nations lacking the sign altogether, but the deeper charge is specifically about Israel's uncircumcised heart.

questions: Jeremiah wished his eyes were a fountain of tears over his people's sin. What griefs in your own life or community do you tend to numb rather than feel honestly before God?
The false prophets cried Peace, peace, when there was no peace. Where might you be settling for comfortable words instead of the harder truth you actually need to hear?
God says the only right boast is knowing Him, not wisdom, strength, or wealth. What do you find yourself most tempted to boast in instead?
Judah carried the sign of the covenant while their hearts remained far from God. Where might your outward religious practice be running ahead of an actual transformed heart?
Jesus wept over Jerusalem exactly as Jeremiah wept centuries before. How does knowing that Christ still grieves over hardened hearts change how you pray for those who resist Him?

translationNotes.note: The phrase balm in Gilead became so memorable that it later entered English idiom and hymnody; tsori refers to a specific medicinal resin for which the region of Gilead was known in trade.

In the Hebrew Bible's chapter division, this verse is actually the final verse of chapter 8, numbered 8:23; English translations including the BSB follow the chapter break adopted in most modern Bibles, so the verse numbers in chapter 9 run one behind the Hebrew text throughout much of the chapter.

The three Hebrew words rendered loving devotion, justice, and righteousness, hesed, mishpat, and tsedaqah, form a compact summary of God's own character that recurs throughout the Prophets and Psalms.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit this session. Sermon search this session found no confirmed match from the trusted preacher cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) specifically on this passage, so sermon status is none.
